ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried beef hor fun. FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (stir-fried noodle dish) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for dry-style beef hor fun in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried beef hor fun or equivalent noodle dish at this specificity level.

main_ingredients: flat rice noodles (hor fun / kway teow)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dry stir-fried beef hor fun is approximately 400 g, typically shared between one to two persons, plated as a single portion of wok-tossed flat rice noodles with sliced beef.

nutrient_source.title: Kway Teow Goreng Daging (Fried Flat Rice Noodles with Beef) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried flat rice noodles with beef (kway teow goreng daging) as the closest approved match. Dry-style (gan chao) preparation is nutritionally comparable to the HPB wet/dry variant; values adapted from HPB per-100g data for this dish category. Sodium may be slightly higher in restaurant dry-style preparations due to concentrated soy sauce coating.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Rice noodles / flat rice noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry-style stir-fried beef hor fun. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles / flat rice noodle dishes (GI range 40–61, central estimate 53), which is the closest approved analogous food. The mixed dish composition (protein, fat, fibre from bean sprouts) further supports a low-GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 70 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 37.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g, a 400 g restaurant serving delivers around 2080 mg sodium — approaching or exceeding the daily limit of 2000 mg recommended by HPB Singapore — so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce preparation, and avoid adding extra soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from flat rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 53 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 37 per full restaurant serving indicate a moderate postprandial glucose impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider sharing the dish or limiting intake to half a serving, and pair it with non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~520 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g) from beef and wok oil poses a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to approximately 200 g, choose lean beef cuts, and request minimal oil preparation to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Beef Hor Fun (Dry), known in Chinese as 干炒牛肉河粉 (Gān Chǎo Niúròu Héfěn), is a classic Cantonese wok dish widely popular in Singapore and Hong Kong. It consists of broad, silky flat rice noodles (hor fun or kway teow) stir-fried at very high heat in a wok with thinly sliced, tenderised beef, bean sprouts, spring onions, and a savoury sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The 'dry' style (干炒) distinguishes it from the 'wet' (湿炒) version: in the dry preparation, no gravy or starchy sauce is added; instead, the noodles are tossed directly in the wok with the sauce ingredients until they develop a characteristic smoky wok-breath aroma (wok hei). The dish has a rich umami flavour, slightly charred edges on the noodles, tender beef, and a satisfying chew. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~148 kcal/100 g), meaningful protein from beef (~7.2 g/100 g), and carbohydrates primarily from rice noodles (~17.5 g/100 g). Sodium content is high (~520 mg/100 g) due to the concentrated soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), but the glycemic load per full restaurant serving is substantial (~37), warranting portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at zi char (煮炒) stalls, Chinese seafood restaurants, and hawker centres, typically as a shared dish for one to two persons.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for dry-style stir-fried beef hor fun as a distinct record. Values are adapted from the closest HPB match (fried flat rice noodles with beef / kway teow goreng daging) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for beef and rice noodle components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for flat rice noodle dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. The dish is served at The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) at The Riverwalk, Singapore, priced at SGD 28.00 for a 1–2 pax portion. Halal status is not confirmed as beef sourcing and preparation method at the specific restaurant have not been verified. The flat aliases list includes all entries from aliases_by_language to ensure consistency. GL per serving calculated using 400 g serving size and 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g: (53 × 70) / 100 = 37.1.
